---
title: "27 · TD Methods Unified: Sarsa, Q, Expected Sarsa"
subtitle: "A single formula relating Sarsa, Q-learning, expected Sarsa, and n-step variants."
lesson-id: "mfrl.td-unified-view"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.q-learning"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Sarsa update from memory? — see [MFRL — 24, Sarsa](../24-sarsa/index.qmd).
2. Q-learning update from memory? — see [MFRL — 26, Q-Learning](../26-q-learning/index.qmd).
3. The on-policy / off-policy distinction (data policy vs target policy)?
4. n-step return $G_t^{(n)}$? — see [MFRL — 25, n-Step Sarsa](../25-n-step-sarsa/index.qmd).

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

You already know three TD algorithms:

| Algorithm | Update target |
|:--|:--|
| **Sarsa** (on-policy) | $r + \gamma \, q(s', a')$, $a' \sim \pi$ |
| **Q-learning** (off-policy) | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a')$ |
| **Expected Sarsa** | $r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') q(s', a')$ |

Look at this widget. Pick a *target* and a *data policy*. The widget tells
you which algorithm you've selected and how it will behave.

```{ojs}
viewof targetType = Inputs.select(["Sarsa: q(s', a')", "Q-learning: max q(s', a')", "Expected Sarsa: E_π[q(s', a')]"], {label: "Target uses:"})
viewof dataPolicy = Inputs.select(["ε-greedy on q", "Uniform random", "Greedy on q (no exploration)"], {label: "Data policy:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let name = "?", onpol = "?", behaviour = "?";
  if (targetType.startsWith("Sarsa")) name = "Sarsa";
  if (targetType.startsWith("Q-learning")) name = "Q-learning";
  if (targetType.startsWith("Expected")) name = "Expected Sarsa";
  if (name === "Q-learning") onpol = "Off-policy (target ignores behaviour)";
  else if (name === "Expected Sarsa" && dataPolicy === "Uniform random") onpol = "Off-policy: target = random's q_π. Equals q^* only if π=π*.";
  else if (name === "Expected Sarsa") onpol = "Variant of on-policy when π is the behaviour policy";
  else onpol = "On-policy (target uses behaviour's a')";
  if (dataPolicy === "Uniform random") behaviour = "Uniform random behaviour ⇒ maximal exploration; coverage guaranteed.";
  else if (dataPolicy === "ε-greedy on q") behaviour = "ε-greedy ⇒ adequate exploration if ε > 0.";
  else behaviour = "Greedy ⇒ no exploration ⇒ fails coverage in general.";
  return html`<div style="font-family: system-ui; padding: 12px; border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px;">
    <p><strong>Algorithm:</strong> ${name}</p>
    <p><strong>On/off-policy:</strong> ${onpol}</p>
    <p><strong>Behaviour:</strong> ${behaviour}</p>
  </div>`;
}
```

Three algorithms, *one master formula*. The choice of "what target?" picks
the algorithm. The choice of "what data policy?" decides whether learning
will be on-policy, off-policy, or coverage-broken.

## Wonder

1. Sarsa, Q-learning, Expected Sarsa look like three different algorithms in
   most textbooks. Could they really all be **one** algorithm with a knob?
2. The Sarsa target is a *random* draw of $a' \sim \pi$. The Expected Sarsa
   target *averages over* $\pi$. The Q-learning target *maxes over* $a'$.
   What do these three operations have in common — what are they all
   *computing*?
3. If your data policy is the same as the target policy, are Sarsa and
   Expected Sarsa estimating the same thing? What's the difference, then?
4. Could you imagine a *fourth* TD method — a different choice of target?

## Discover

Five problems to compress three algorithms into one master formula and feel
the design space.

### Problem 1 — the master form

Write all three TD updates side-by-side and find the common shape.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$q(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; q(s, a) + \alpha \, [\,T - q(s, a)\,],$$
where $T$ is one of:
- **Sarsa:** $T = r + \gamma q(s', a')$, $a' \sim \pi$.
- **Q-learning:** $T = r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a')$.
- **Expected Sarsa:** $T = r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') q(s', a')$.

The recursion is identical; only $T$ changes.
</details>

### Problem 2 — three operations on $q(s', \cdot)$

Each algorithm processes $q(s', \cdot)$ — a vector of action values at $s'$ —
into a single number for the target. Sarsa *samples*, Q-learning *maxes*,
Expected Sarsa *averages*. Why are these the natural three?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What does each operation correspond to under the Bellman equation?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The Bellman equation has the structure $v(s) = \mathbb{E}_a[\,q(s, a)\,]$ for
some policy distribution. Three ways to compute that expectation:
- **Sample one $a'$** (Sarsa) — unbiased estimator of the on-policy Bellman
  expectation.
- **Take the average** $\sum \pi(a') q(s', a')$ (Expected Sarsa) — exact
  expectation under $\pi$, lower variance.
- **Take the max** (Q-learning) — exact expectation under the *optimal*
  policy.

Sample, average, max — three ways of collapsing $q(s', \cdot)$ to a number.
</details>

### Problem 3 — bias-variance ranking

Order Sarsa, Q-learning, Expected Sarsa by per-step *variance*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **Sarsa:** randomness from $a' \sim \pi$ AND from $r, s'$ — highest
  variance.
- **Expected Sarsa:** averages out the $a'$ randomness — lower variance.
- **Q-learning:** no $a'$ randomness — lowest "policy-side" variance.
  *But* maximisation bias adds noise of its own.

In practice **Expected Sarsa often wins** in tabular settings because it
keeps Sarsa's on-policy guarantee while killing the $a'$-variance.
</details>

### Problem 4 — what target ⇒ what fixed point

Sarsa converges to $q_\pi$. Q-learning converges to $q^\*$. What does
Expected Sarsa converge to?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The expected-target operator's fixed point.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Expected Sarsa's expected target is exactly the same as Sarsa's expected
target, *under the same $\pi$*: $\mathbb{E}[r + \gamma q(s', a')] = \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{a'}[q(s', a')]]$. So **Expected Sarsa
converges to $q_\pi$** for the policy $\pi$ used in the target. If $\pi$ is
the behaviour policy, it's on-policy; if $\pi$ is *another* policy (such as
the greedy policy under the current $q$), it becomes a flexible *general
policy iteration* engine.
</details>

### Problem 5 — n-step versions

The n-step Sarsa update from Lesson 25 used $G_t^{(n)}$ as target.
What are the n-step analogues of Q-learning and Expected Sarsa?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Replace the bootstrap term at step $t + n$ with the corresponding "max" or
"expectation" operation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **n-step Q-learning:** $G_t^{(n), Q} = r_{t+1} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n} + \gamma^n \max_{a'} q(s_{t+n}, a')$. Off-policy in the
  *bootstrap*, but the intermediate trajectory was generated on-policy
  — needing **importance sampling** corrections to fix this in the strict
  off-policy case.
- **n-step Expected Sarsa:** $G_t^{(n), \text{ESarsa}} = r_{t+1} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n} + \gamma^n \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s_{t+n}) q(s_{t+n}, a')$.

These appear in the literature as **Tree-Backup** and **Q($\sigma$)** with
$\sigma$ a per-step knob between sampling and expectation. Don't worry about
the names; the design space is one knob.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
Every TD method is **"target − current estimate, scaled by $\alpha$."** The
differences are entirely in *what target you use*. The choice of target is
the algorithm. Once you internalise this, the literature stops being a
zoo of named algorithms and becomes a small design space.
:::

::: {.fun-fact}
**Expected Sarsa** has an unusual history. It was independently proposed
multiple times in the 1990s and early 2000s without becoming famous, until
**van Seijen, van Hasselt, Whiteson, Wiering** wrote a 2009 ICML paper
titled "A theoretical and empirical analysis of Expected Sarsa" that
unified all the prior proposals and showed it consistently beat plain Sarsa
on tabular benchmarks. The lesson: **good ideas without a benchmark study
get rediscovered for a decade.** That paper finally settled it.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Master TD update.** Given $(s, a, r, s')$:
$$
q(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; q(s, a) + \alpha \, [\,T(s', \cdot) - q(s, a)\,],
$$
where $T$ is the target value. Three canonical choices:

| Algorithm | Target $T$ at next state | Fixed point | On/off |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Sarsa** | $r + \gamma \, q(s', a')$ | $q_\pi$ | on-policy |
| **Q-learning** | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a')$ | $q^\*$ | off-policy |
| **Expected Sarsa** | $r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a'\mid s') q(s', a')$ | $q_\pi$ | depends on $\pi$ |

The choice of $T$ — *sample*, *max*, or *expectation* over $q(s', \cdot)$ —
is the entire design knob.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Sample one action — Sarsa is born.*
> *Take the maximum — Q-learning rises.*
> *Average over policy — Expected Sarsa awakens.*
> *One recursion, three faces.*

## Apply

### Worked example — three targets, one transition

> A grid: $(s, a, r, s') = (s_3, \text{right}, +0.5, s_4)$. Action values at
> $s_4$: $q(s_4, \text{up}) = 1$, $q(s_4, \text{down}) = 2$,
> $q(s_4, \text{left}) = 0$, $q(s_4, \text{right}) = 3$. Policy at $s_4$:
> $\pi(\cdot \mid s_4) = (0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.7)$ (greedy on right with ε=0.4
> spread). $\gamma = 0.9$. Compute the three targets.

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. **Sarsa target.** Suppose the agent samples $a' = \text{right}$ (the
   greedy pick under ε-greedy). Target $= 0.5 + 0.9 \cdot q(s_4, \text{right}) = 0.5 + 2.7 = 3.2$. (If they had sampled $a' = \text{up}$ instead, the
   target would be $0.5 + 0.9 = 1.4$ — sample variance!)
2. **Q-learning target.** $\max_{a'} q(s_4, a') = 3$. Target $= 0.5 + 0.9 \cdot 3 = 3.2$. Always the same; no sample.
3. **Expected Sarsa target.** $\sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s_4) q(s_4, a') = 0.1 \cdot 1 + 0.1 \cdot 2 + 0.1 \cdot 0 + 0.7 \cdot 3 = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0 + 2.1 = 2.4$.
   Target $= 0.5 + 0.9 \cdot 2.4 = 2.66$.

Same transition, three different targets. Sarsa happens to match Q-learning
in this case because the agent sampled the greedy action — but that's luck.
Expected Sarsa is *between* the two, weighted by $\pi$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute Q-learning and Expected Sarsa targets for $(r, \gamma) = (0, 0.95)$ with $q(s', \cdot) = (4, 6, 5)$ and $\pi(\cdot \mid s') = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Q-learning target** $= 0 + 0.95 \cdot 6 = 5.7$.
**Expected Sarsa target** $= 0 + 0.95 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 4 + 0.3 \cdot 6 + 0.2 \cdot 5) = 0.95 \cdot 4.8 = 4.56$.
</details>

**W2.** True or false: "Expected Sarsa with the *greedy* policy as $\pi$ is
the same as Q-learning."

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**True.** If $\pi$ puts probability 1 on $\arg\max_{a'} q(s', a')$, the
expectation $\sum \pi q$ collapses to $\max q$. So Q-learning is the
"Expected Sarsa with greedy target" special case.
</details>

**W3.** Order the three algorithms by target *variance* on a fixed transition.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Q-learning** (zero) ≈ **Expected Sarsa** (zero) < **Sarsa** (positive,
from sampling $a'$). Both Q-learning and Expected Sarsa eliminate the $a'$
variance. The remaining variance comes from $r, s'$, which all three share.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that **Q-learning is Expected Sarsa with $\pi$ = greedy(q)**.
What's subtle: the $\pi$ here changes as $q$ changes.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
With $\pi(\arg\max_{a'} q(s', a') \mid s') = 1$ and 0 elsewhere,
$\sum \pi q = \max q$. So target ≡ Q-learning's. The subtle point: this
$\pi$ is *not* a fixed policy — it's the greedy-on-$q$ policy, which
*moves* as $q$ updates. So Q-learning's effective "target policy" is
*always* the greedy improvement of the current $q$. That's policy iteration
in disguise.
</details>

**P2.** Implement the three targets on a 1-step transition tuple by Python
pseudo-code. Highlight where the algorithms differ.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
```
def sarsa_target(r, gamma, q, sp, ap):
    return r + gamma * q[sp][ap]

def qlearning_target(r, gamma, q, sp):
    return r + gamma * max(q[sp])

def expected_sarsa_target(r, gamma, q, sp, pi):
    return r + gamma * sum(pi[sp][a] * q[sp][a] for a in range(A))
```
The functional difference is one line each: `q[sp][ap]` vs `max(q[sp])`
vs `sum(pi[sp] * q[sp])`. Everything else — buffer, update, schedule — is
identical.
</details>

**P3.** *Why is Expected Sarsa's *update* an Off-policy method when $\pi$
is not the behaviour policy?* Sketch.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The behaviour generated $a$; the target uses an explicit policy distribution
$\pi$ that need not match.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The target $\sum_{a'} \pi(a'\mid s') q(s', a')$ uses an *explicit* policy
$\pi$, irrespective of which $a'$ the behaviour would have sampled. So if
$\pi$ ≠ behaviour, the algorithm is off-policy: data from one policy,
evaluation of another. You don't even need importance sampling for this
form, because the sum *itself* takes care of the policy weighting. This is
why Expected Sarsa is favoured for off-policy *evaluation* in practice.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Design a fourth TD method.* Pick any operation
$\Phi: q(s', \cdot) \to \mathbb{R}$ and write the resulting algorithm. State
its fixed point.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try a *softmax* or a *mean of top-k* operation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Example: **Softmax-Sarsa** with target $r + \gamma \cdot \text{softmax}_\beta(q(s', \cdot))$ where $\text{softmax}_\beta(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{\beta} \log \sum_{a'} \exp(\beta q(s', a'))$. As $\beta \to \infty$ this recovers
Q-learning's max; as $\beta \to 0$ it recovers a uniform average. The fixed
point is $q$ for the softmax-greedy policy with temperature $1/\beta$. This
is **soft Q-learning** and underpins maximum-entropy RL methods like SAC.
The design space of "what operation to apply at the bootstrap" is rich.
</details>

**S2.** *Why doesn't Sarsa's variance ruin tabular learning?* Sketch.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Variance per *update* matters; over many updates it averages out.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sarsa's per-update variance is bounded by $\sigma_{a'}^2 \cdot \gamma^2$
plus the inherent $r, s'$ variance. Robbins-Monro / Dvoretzky averages this
out *across* updates, killing the variance contribution as
$\sum \alpha_t^2 \cdot \mathrm{Var}$. Vanishing $\alpha_t$ wins in the
limit. Expected Sarsa just *starts* with less variance, so it gets to a
given precision with fewer updates.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Q-learning, Sarsa, Expected Sarsa are different algorithms."** They
   are *the same* update with different targets. Pick the target that
   matches your goal (estimate $q^\*$ vs $q_\pi$) and your variance budget.
2. **"Expected Sarsa is always better than Sarsa."** Better in tabular when
   variance matters. With function approximation Expected Sarsa is more
   expensive per update (you must sum over actions); the trade is real.
3. **"Q-learning is just Sarsa with $a'$ = greedy."** Almost — except
   that *substitution* is what makes it off-policy. The greedy action is
   not necessarily the action *taken*; it's the action *valued*. That
   subtle "valued not taken" is the whole story.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else:

1. **One master form:** $q \leftarrow q + \alpha [T - q]$.
2. **Three canonical targets:** sample (Sarsa), max (Q-learning), expectation
   (Expected Sarsa). Pick by goal and variance budget.
3. **The literature's algorithm zoo is a small design space.** Once you
   see the master form, every new TD variant becomes "ah, that's a
   different choice of $T$."

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state the master TD update and three canonical targets without
   notes?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without peeking past
   *Hint 1*?
3. Can you spot a "new" TD algorithm in a paper and immediately identify
   *which target* it's using?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Lesson 28 — From Table to Function](../28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S1 — designing your own TD variant tests whether
  the master form has clicked.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read the *Name* table. The whole lesson collapses to it.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[MFRL — 24, Sarsa](../24-sarsa/index.qmd)** — sampling target.
- **[MFRL — 26, Q-Learning](../26-q-learning/index.qmd)** — max target.
- **[MFRL — 25, n-Step Sarsa](../25-n-step-sarsa/index.qmd)** — the
  $n$-step extension axis.

### What this unlocks

- **[MFRL — 28, From Table to Function](../28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd)** — the master form survives function approximation; only $q$ is now
  $\hat q(s, a; w)$.
- **[MFRL — 31, DQN](../31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)** — Q-learning target
  with deep-network $\hat q$.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Modern algorithms like Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) use a softmax
target — *another* point in the same design space. **Maximum-entropy RL**
is "Expected Sarsa with a softmax policy"; **DQN variants** are "Q-learning
target with engineering tricks." The unifying view in this lesson is the
view practitioners use when they read a new paper.
See [Bellman equation](/glossary.qmd#bellman-equation) and
[expected value](/glossary.qmd#expected-value).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Researchers often design new TD methods by varying the target.
The 2018 paper *Distributional Reinforcement Learning* (Bellemare et al.)
varies it most radically — replacing scalar $q$ with a *distribution* over
returns and updating the distribution via a Wasserstein-style target.
Same master form, distributional target.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands into 40 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Source:** Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement
  Learning* (Springer 2025), Chapter 7 §7.5. Cited freely; never transcribed.
- **Touchstones honoured:** Russian school problem-first sequencing,
  Better Explained "one design knob" framing, Bhāskara verse for the
  three faces.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (table, recursion, English, code) ✓, graduated hints ✓, worked
  solutions ✓, common-confusions ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
